# Understanding the Thalidomide Chirality in Biological Processes by the Self-disproportionation of Enantiomers

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 8**, Article number: 17131 (2018)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

88k Accesses

128 Citations

65 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Twenty years after the thalidomide disaster in the late 1950s, Blaschke *et al*. reported that only the (*S*)-enantiomer of thalidomide is teratogenic. However, other work has shown that the enantiomers of thalidomide interconvert *in vivo*, which begs the question: why is teratogen activity not observed in animal experiments that use (*R*)-thalidomide given the ready *in vivo* racemization (“thalidomide paradox”)? Herein, we disclose a hypothesis to explain this “thalidomide paradox” through the *in-vivo* self-disproportionation of enantiomers. Upon stirring a 20% ee solution of thalidomide in a given solvent, significant enantiomeric enrichment of up to 98% ee was observed reproducibly in solution. We hypothesize that a fraction of thalidomide enantiomers epimerizes *in vivo*, followed by precipitation of racemic thalidomide in (*R*/*S*)-heterodimeric form. Thus, racemic thalidomide is most likely removed from biological processes upon racemic precipitation in (*R*/*S*)-heterodimeric form. On the other hand, enantiomerically pure thalidomide remains in solution, affording the observed biological experimental results: the (*S*)-enantiomer is teratogenic, while the (*R*)-enantiomer is not.

## Introduction

Thalidomide is one of the most notorious drugs, responsible for a tragic global medical disaster of limb malformations in the late 1950s[1](#ref-CR1 "Burley, D. M. & Lenz, W. Thalidomide and congenital abnormalities. Lancet 279, 271–272 (1962)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Lenz, W. A short history of thalidomide embryopathy. Teratology 38, 203–215 (1988)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Diggle, G. E. Thalidomide: 40 years on. Int J Clin Pract 55, 627–631 (2001)."),[4](#ref-CR4 "Stephens, T. D. Proposed mechanisms of action in thalidomide embryopathy. Teratology 38, 229–239 (1988)."),[5](#ref-CR5 "Bartlett, J. B., Dredge, K. & Dalgleish, A. G. The evolution of thalidomide and its IMiD derivatives as anticancer agents. Nat. Rev. Cancer 4, 314 (2004)."),[6](#ref-CR6 "Wu, J. J., Huang, D. B., Pang, K. R., Hsu, S. & Tyring, S. K. Thalidomide: dermatological indications, mechanisms of action and side-effects. Br. J. Dermatol 153, 254–273 (2005)."),[7](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR7 "Melchert, M. & List, A. The thalidomide saga. Int. J. Biochem. Cell Biol. 39, 1489–1499 (2007)."). Thalidomide had mainly been prescribed as a sleeping drug/sedative, but also to pregnant women for the relief of morning sickness. However, the increased number of limb malformations was later confirmed to be caused by thalidomide. Although thalidomide was banned in 1962 in Germany, and later worldwide, it has once again attracted clinical interest due to the discovery of its unique pharmacological action against a number of intractable diseases, such as leprosy, human immunodeficiency virus replication in acquired immune deficiency syndrome, and cancer[8](#ref-CR8 "Matthews, S. J. & McCoy, C. Thalidomide: A review of approved and investigational uses. Clin. Ther. 25, 342–395 (2003)."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Hashimoto, Y., Tanatani, A., Nagasawa, K. & Miyachi, H. Thalidomide as a multi-target drug and its application as a template for drug design. Drugs Future 29, 383–391 (2004)."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Franks, M. E., Macpherson, G. R. & Figg, W. D. Thalidomide. Lancet 363, 1802–1811 (2004)."),[11](#ref-CR11 "Brennen, W. N., Cooper, C. R., Capitosti, S., Brown, M. L. & Sikes, R. A. Thalidomide and analogues: current proposed mechanisms and therapeutic usage. Clin. Prostate Cancer 3, 54–61 (2004)."),[12](#ref-CR12 "Luzzio, F. A. & Figg, W. D. Thalidomide analogues: derivatives of an orphan drug with diverse biological activity. Expert Opin. Ther. Pat. 14, 215–229 (2004)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Sleijfer, S., Kruit, W. H. & Stoter, G. Thalidomide in solid tumours: the resurrection of an old drug. Eur. J. Cancer 40, 2377–2382 (2004)."),[14](#ref-CR14 "Kumar, S., Witzig, T. E. & Rajkumar, S. V. Thalidomide: Current Role in the Treatment of Non-Plasma Cell Malignancies. J. Clin. Oncol. 22, 2477–2488 (2004)."),[15](#ref-CR15 "Hashimoto, Y. Thalidomide as a Multi-Template for Development of Biologically Active Compounds. Arch. Pharm. 341, 536–547 (2008)."),[16](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR16 "Knobloch, J. & Rüther, U. Shedding light on an old mystery: Thalidomide suppresses survival pathways to induce limb defects. Cell Cycle 7, 1121–1127 (2008)."). Thalidomide is now on the market with the new brand name THALOMID® for the treatment of patients with diagnosed multiple myeloma and severe erythema nodosum leprosum.

Thalidomide (**1**) possesses a single stereogenic carbon center and thus (*S*)- and (*R*)-enantiomers (Fig. [1](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig1)). Commercially, it was marketed as a racemate. In 1979, Blaschke *et al*. discovered that the enantiomers of **1** display different biological properties and that only the (*S*)-enantiomer of **1** is responsible for the teratogenic side effects, while no teratogenicity was observed for (*R*)-**1** in animal experiments[17](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR17 "Blaschke, G., Kraft, H. P., Fickentscher, K. & Kohler, F. Chromatographic separation of racemic thalidomide and teratogenic activity of its enantiomers. Arzneim.-Forsch. 29, 1640–1642 (1979)."). This paper suggested that the thalidomide disaster could have been avoided if only (*R*)-**1** had been marketed instead of racemic **1**. However, it is uncertain whether any of the actions of racemic thalidomide (**1**) could be avoided by using a pure enantiomer of **1**, due to the considerable racemization observed after incubation of enantiomerically pure **1** in buffer solution (τ1/2 = 12 h)[18](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR18 "Nishimura, K., Hashimoto, Y. & Iwasaki, S. (S)-form of alpha-methyl-N(alpha)-phthalimidoglutarimide, but not its (R)-form, enhanced phorbol ester-induced tumor necrosis factor-alpha production by human leukemia cell HL-60: implication of optical resolution of thalidomidal effects. Chem. Pharm. Bull. 42, 1157–1159 (1994)."),[19](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR19 "Wnendt, S. et al. Enantioselective inhibition of TNF-alpha release by thalidomide and thalidomide-analogues. Chirality 8, 390–396 (1996).") as well as in serum (τ1/2 = 1 h)[20](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR20 "Knoche, B. & Blaschke, G. Investigations on the in vitro racemization of thalidomide by high-performance liquid chromatography. J. Chromatogr. A 666, 235–240 (1994)."). Thus, how can these results be rationalized in the light of the findings by Blaschke *et al*., which confirmed that (*R*)-**1** is not teratogenic despite the *in vivo* racemization? Although several enantioselective biological studies on the metabolism of thalidomide have been reported[21](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR21 "Meyring, M. et al. In vitro biotransformation of (R)- and (S)-thalidomide: application of circular dichroism spectroscopy to the stereochemical characterization of the hydroxylated metabolites. Anal. Chem. 74, 3726–3735 (2002)."), the confirmation of the different biological activity of the pure thalidomide enantiomers has ultimately remained problematic. In 2010, Handa and co-workers carried out a landmark biological study on thalidomide by identifying cereblon (CRBN) as a thalidomide-binding protein[22](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR22 "Ito, T. et al. Identification of a Primary Target of Thalidomide Teratogenicity. Science 327, 1345–1350 (2010)."),[23](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR23 "Chamberlain, P. P. et al. Structure of the human Cereblon-DDB1-lenalidomide complex reveals basis for responsiveness to thalidomide analogs. Nat. Struct. Mol. Biol. 21, 803–809 (2014)."). They found that thalidomide induces its teratogenic effects by binding to CRBN in zebrafish and chicks. This report opened a new era for thalidomide in science and completely redirected the research on this drug[24](#ref-CR24 "Ito, T. & Handa, H. Cereblon and its downstream substrates as molecular targets of immunomodulatory drugs. Int. J. Hematol. 104, 293–299 (2016)."),[25](#ref-CR25 "Ito, T., Ando, H. & Handa, H. Teratogenic effects of thalidomide: molecular mechanisms. Cell. Mol. Life Sci. 68, 1569–1579 (2011)."),[26](#ref-CR26 "Ito, T. & Handa, H. Another action of a thalidomide derivative. Nature 523, 167 (2015)."),[27](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR27 "Ottis, P. & Crews, C. M. Proteolysis-Targeting Chimeras: Induced Protein Degradation as a Therapeutic Strategy. ACS Chem. Biol. 12, 892–898 (2017)."). In 2018, Hakoshima, Handa, and co-workers, including two of the authors of the present study, finally put an end to the debate on the teratogenicity of (*S*)-thalidomide via structural and biochemical studies of the (*S*)- and (*R*)-enantiomers of thalidomide coordinated to CRBN[28](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR28 "Mori, T. et al. Structural basis of thalidomide enantiomer binding to cereblon. Sci. Rep. 8, 1294 (2018)."). The biochemical studies were carried out on deuterium-substituted enantiomers of thalidomide[29](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR29 "Yamamoto, T., Tokunaga, E., Nakamura, S., Shibata, N. & Toru, T. Synthesis and configurational stability of (S)- and (R)-deuteriothalidomides. Chem. Pharm. Bull. 58, 110–112 (2010).") to suppress any enantiomeric interconversion. The results revealed that the (*S*)-enantiomer of **1** displayed a 10-fold stronger binding to CRBN and inhibition of self-ubiquitylation compared to the (*R*)-isomer. The crystal structure of the thalidomide-binding domain of CRBN bound to each of the enantiomers revealed that both bind to the CRBN pocket, although the bound form of the (*S*)-enantiomer exhibits a more natural ring conformation of the six-membered glutarimide moiety. Thus, the report by Blaschke is absolutely correct, i.e., the teratogenic effects are induced exclusively by the (*S*)-enantiomer of thalidomide.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-018-35457-6/MediaObjects/41598_2018_35457_Fig1_HTML.png)

(*S*)- and (*R*)-enantiomers of thalidomide (**1**).

However, one question remains regarding the clear biological differences between the thalidomide enantiomers reported in 1979, despite the observed racemization of thalidomide. Why do animal experiments that use (*R*)-**1** not display teratogenicity if (*R*)-**1** readily racemizes *in vivo*? This discrepancy is known as the “thalidomide paradox”. During our continuous studies on **1** and its derivatives[30](#ref-CR30 "Takeuchi, Y., Shiragami, T., Kimura, K., Suzuki, E. & Shibata, N. (R)- and (S)-3-Fluorothalidomides:  Isosteric Analogues of Thalidomide. Org. Lett. 1, 1571–1573 (1999)."),[31](#ref-CR31 "Suzuki, E. & Shibata, N. Three-step synthesis of (R)- and (S)-thalidomides from ornithine. Enantiomer 6, 275–279 (2001)."),[32](#ref-CR32 "Shibata, N., Yamamoto, T & Toru, T. In Topics in Heterocyclic Chemistry, ed. Eguchi, S. 8, 73–97 (Springer, Berlin, Heidelberg, 2007)."),[33](#ref-CR33 "Yamamoto, T. et al. Enzymatic resolution and evaluation of enantiomers of cis-5′-hydroxythalidomide. Org. Biomol. Chem. 6, 1540–1543 (2008)."),[34](#ref-CR34 "Yamamoto, T. et al. Synthesis, configurational stability and stereochemical biological evaluations of (S)- and (R)-5-hydroxythalidomides. Bioorg. Med. Chem. Lett. 19, 3973–3976 (2009)."),[35](#ref-CR35 "Suzuki, S. et al. Design and Synthesis of Thalidomide–Deoxyribonucleoside Chimeras. Chem. Lett. 38, 1046–1047 (2009)."),[36](#ref-CR36 "Lee, C. J., Shibata, N., Wiley, M. J. & Wells, P. G. Fluorothalidomide: a characterization of maternal and developmental toxicity in rabbits and mice. Toxicol. Sci. 122, 157–169 (2011)."),[37](#ref-CR37 "Yamamoto, T., Suzuki, Y., Ito, E., Tokunaga, E. & Shibata, N. Asymmetric Synthesis of Both Mirror Images of 3′-Fluorothalidomide by Enantiodivergent Fluorination Using a Single, Cinchona Alkaloid. Org. Lett. 13, 470–473 (2011)."),[38](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR38 "Tokunaga, E. et al. Biological evaluation of both enantiomers of fluoro-thalidomide using human myeloma cell line H929 and others. PLoS One 12, e0182152 (2017)."), we noticed that the physicochemical properties of racemic **1** and its pure enantiomers are very different[39](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR39 "Maeno, M. et al. Self-disproportionation of enantiomers of thalidomide and its fluorinated analogue via gravity-driven achiral chromatography: mechanistic rationale and implications. Chem. Sci. 6, 1043–1048 (2015)."). Herein, we disclose a hypothesis to explain the “thalidomide paradox” based on the self-disproportionation of the enantiomers of non-racemic thalidomide in biological processes.

## Results

The term self-disproportionation of enantiomers (SDE) was introduced by Soloshonok in 2006 to describe the transformation of an enantiomerically enriched system through the formation of fractions with a different proportion of enantiomers relative to the original ratio[40](#ref-CR40 "Soloshonok, V. A. Remarkable Amplification of the Self-Disproportionation of Enantiomers on Achiral-Phase Chromatography Columns. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 45, 766–769 (2006)."),[41](#ref-CR41 "Soloshonok, V. A. et al. Phenomenon of Optical Self-Purification of Chiral Non-Racemic Compounds. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 129, 12112–12113 (2007)."),[42](#ref-CR42 "Soloshonok, V. A., Roussel, C., Kitagawa, O. & Sorochinsky, A. E. Self-disproportionation of enantiomers via achiral chromatography: a warning and an extra dimension in optical purifications. Chem. Soc. Rev. 41, 4180–4188 (2012)."),[43](#ref-CR43 "Han, J., Kitagawa, O., Wzorek, A., Klika, K. D. & Soloshonok, V. A. The self-disproportionation of enantiomers (SDE): a menace or an opportunity? Chem. Sci. 9, 1718–1739 (2018)."),[44](#ref-CR44 "Ueki, T., Yasumoto, M. & Soloshonok, V. A. Rational application of self-disproportionation of enantiomers via sublimation - a novel methodological dimension for enantiomeric purifications. Tetrahedron: Asymmetry 21, 1396–1400 (2010)."),[45](#ref-CR45 "Sorochinsky, A. E., Aceña, J. L. & Soloshonok, V. A. Self-Disproportionation of Enantiomers of Chiral, Non-Racemic Fluoroorganic Compounds: Role of Fluorine as Enabling Element. Synthesis 45, 141–152 (2013)."),[46](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR46 "Sorochinsky, A. E. et al. Optical Purifications via Self‐Disproportionation of Enantiomers by Achiral Chromatography: Case Study of a Series of α‐CF3‐containing Secondary Alcohols. Chirality 25, 365–368 (2013)."). Our investigation began by examining whether SDE would occur in a solution of **1**. A solid mixture of (*R*)- and (*S*)-**1** (*R*/*S* = ~3 mg/2 mg) was dissolved in a variety of organic solvents, the enantiomeric excess (ee) was determined, and then water (1.0 mL) was added. In these solutions, the progressive formation of a precipitate was observed. After 1 h, the ee of **1** in the supernatant was determined by HPLC using a chiral column (Fig. [2](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig2), Table [1](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab1)). In EtOH (8 mL), the initial ee of (*R*)-**1** was 21%, while the final supernatant showed 22% ee of (*R*)-**1**, (entry 1, Table [1](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab1)). A similar result was obtained in CHCl3/EtOH (1:1, 4 mL, entry 2). Interestingly, the SDE of **1** was also observed in DMSO (entries 3–4). The use of 0.2 mL of DMSO as the organic solvent resulted in an increased enantiomeric excess of (*R*)-**1** (50% ee) in the supernatant relative to the initial value (16% ee, entry 3). After optimization of the volume, 25 μL of DMSO was found to be effective for this SDE: an initial 19% ee of (*R*)-**1** afforded enantioenriched (*R*)-**1** with 83% ee in the supernatant, while a substantial amount of precipitate was also observed (entry 4). Further details for the applied conditions are shown in the Supplementary Information (Table [S1](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#MOESM1)).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-018-35457-6/MediaObjects/41598_2018_35457_Fig2_HTML.png)

Self-disproportionation of non-racemic **1** (for Table [1](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab1)).

Next, experiments under conditions simulating biological media were carried out, i.e., without organic solvents. Solid (*R*)- and (*S*)-**1** (*R*/*S* = 3 mg/2 mg) were mixed and ground by pestle, before water (1 mL) was added (Fig. [3](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig3), Table [2](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab2)). After vigorous stirring for 1 h at room temperature, the supernatant of the suspension contained 97% ee of (*R*)-**1** (entry 1, Table [2](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab2)). High enantiomeric excess values for (*R*)-**1** (97% and 91% ee) were also obtained after stirring at 37 °C for 1 and 24 h, respectively (entries 2 and 3). The use of phosphate buffer (pH = 7) as the solvent at room temperature provided (*R*)-**1** with a similarly high enantiomeric excess (98% and 89% ee) independently of the stirring time (entries 4 and 5). At 37 °C in phosphate buffer, 98% ee of (*R*)-**1** was detected in the supernatant after 1 h, which decreased to 62% ee after 24 h of stirring (entries 6 and 7). This phenomenon was also observed for (*S*)-**1**, which provided enantioenriched (*S*)-**1** (89% ee) under the same conditions (entry 8). Other ratios of (*R*)- and (*S*)-**1** were also examined in water and similar behavior was observed (entries 9–11). In all cases, a substantial amount of precipitate was observed in the final solution, and the precipitate showed a low ee of **1** (*vide infra*). Further details for the different conditions applied are shown in the Supplementary Information (Table [S2](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#MOESM1)).

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-018-35457-6/MediaObjects/41598_2018_35457_Fig3_HTML.png)

Self-disproportionation of non-racemic **1** in water and phosphate buffer (pH = 7) (for Table [2](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab2)).

Subsequently, we attempted to carry out the SDE of non-racemic thalidomide at a practical scale (Fig. [4](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig4)). For that purpose, (*R*)-**1** (50 mg, 20% ee) was dissolved in DMSO (0.25 mL or 2 mL) before water (27 mL) was added. After stirring for 1 h at room temperature, the formed precipitate was filtered off. The filtrate and precipitate were dried separately by freeze drying. The filtrate afforded (*R*)-**1** (3.7 mg) with 78% ee, while the precipitate contained 19% ee of (*R*)-**1**. This experiment was repeated three times and similar results were obtained.

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-018-35457-6/MediaObjects/41598_2018_35457_Fig4_HTML.png)

Self-disproportionation of non-racemic thalidomide at practical scale; aDMSO (0.25 mL) was used.

Then, we examined the solubility of enantiomeric and racemic **1**. The solubility of **1** in water was determined by absorption spectroscopy. For that purpose, a saturated solution of **1** in water was prepared by stirring enantiomeric or racemic **1** (10 mg) in water (5 mL) at room temperature (23 °C) for 1 h. After filtering off all insoluble solids, the saturated water solution of **1** (1.0 mL) was diluted to 100 mL, before the UV spectrum was recorded. The water solubility of **1** was calculated using a calibration curve. The water solubility of (*S*)-**1** (348.9 ± 2.9 μg/mL) and (*R*)-**1** (344.9 ± 3.0 μg/mL) was found to be 5.5 times higher than that of racemate-**1** (62.5 ± 1.6 μg/mL).

Such differences in the solubility of the individual enantiomers and the racemate of thalidomide **1** are in good agreement with the differences in their X-ray crystal structures that have previously been reported by us[39](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR39 "Maeno, M. et al. Self-disproportionation of enantiomers of thalidomide and its fluorinated analogue via gravity-driven achiral chromatography: mechanistic rationale and implications. Chem. Sci. 6, 1043–1048 (2015)."),[47](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR47 "Suzuki, T. et al. Evaluation of stability difference between asymmetric homochiral dimer in (S)-thalidomide crystal and symmetric heterochiral dimer in (RS)-thalidomide crystal. Phase Transitions 83, 223–234 (2010)."). The crystal structure of (*S*)-**1** shows two types of arrangements: a solvated monomer and a non-solvated homodimer of two (*S*)-**1** molecules with hydrogen-bonding at the glutarimide rings. On the other hand, a non-solvated heterodimer of (*R*)- and (*S*)-thalidomide molecules was obtained for racemic-**1**. Obviously, the solvated monomer of (*S*)-**1** is more soluble than the other structures. Moreover, the homodimer of (*S*)-**1** should be more soluble than the heterodimer of racemic **1**, as the latter is more closely packed on account of two intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Namely, the lengths of two intermolecular hydrogen bonds of (*S/S*)-homodimer **1** are 2.912(3) Å and 2.910(3) Å, while those of racemic (*R*/*S*)-heterodimer **1** are 2.895(4) Å (Fig. [5](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig5)).

![figure 5](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-018-35457-6/MediaObjects/41598_2018_35457_Fig5_HTML.png)

Structures of the (*S/S*)-homodimer and the (*R/S*)-heterodimer of **1** based on X-ray crystallographic analyses.

## Discussion

The SDE of non-racemic thalidomide in water (Tables [1](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab1) and [2](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Tab2) as well as Fig. [4](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig4)) can be explained by the differences in the solubility of the enantiomers and the racemate of **1**. First, (*R*)-**1** in low ee gradually undergoes disproportionation into enantiomerically pure (*R*)-**1** (as a monomer and (*R*/*R*)-homodimer) and racemate-**1** (as an (*R*/*S*)-heterodimer). Due to the low solubility of the (*R*/*S*)-heterodimer, racemate-**1** precipitates, while enantiomerically enriched (*R*)-**1** remains in solution (Fig. [6a](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig6)). The SDE of (*S*)-**1** can be explained in the same way. This phenomenon of racemic precipitation of the (*R*/*S*)-heterodimer could have some relations to asymmetric amplification reactions in which (*R*/*S*)-heterodimers have reduced catalytic activity relative to that of enantiomerically pure monomeric catalysts[48](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR48 "Amplification of Chirality, Top. Curr. Chem. ed. Soai, K., 284 (Springer, Berlin, Heidelberg, 2008)."),[49](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR49 "Shibata, N. et al. Highly Enantioselective Catalytic Fluorination and Chlorination Reactions of Carbonyl Compounds Capable of Two-Point Binding. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 44, 4204–4207 (2005).").

![figure 6](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-018-35457-6/MediaObjects/41598_2018_35457_Fig6_HTML.png)

(**a**) Mechanism of the SDE for (*R*)-**1**. (**b**) An SDE-based hypothesis that feasibly explains why (*R*)-**1** does not show any teratogenic effect *in vivo*.

The results by Blaschke *et al*.[17](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR17 "Blaschke, G., Kraft, H. P., Fickentscher, K. & Kohler, F. Chromatographic separation of racemic thalidomide and teratogenic activity of its enantiomers. Arzneim.-Forsch. 29, 1640–1642 (1979).") are thus most likely correct, provided that the SDE of thalidomide occurs in living biological systems. First, the acidic hydrogen atom at the asymmetric center of enantiomerically pure (*R*)-**1** epimerizes under physiological conditions, resulting in the formation of enantiomerically poor (*R*)-**1** (e.g. 20% ee). Such enantiomer mixtures of thalidomide with low ee spontaneously undergo a SDE to provide enantiopure (*R*)-**1** and racemic **1**. Racemic **1** is then removed from the biological medium by precipitation, while enantiopure (*R*)-**1** remains in solution to reach the target cells and/or organs (Fig. [6b](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig6)). This hypothesis is also supported by the fact that the oral absorption of racemic thalidomide is slower than that of the individual thalidomide enantiomers[50](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR50 "Eriksson, T., Bjöurkman, S., Roth, B., Fyge, Å. & Höuglund, P. Stereospecific determination, chiral inversion in vitro and pharmacokinetics in humans of the enantiomers of thalidomide. Chirality 7, 44–52 (1995)."),[51](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR51 "Eriksson, T., Bjorkman, S. & Hoglund, P. Clinical pharmacology of thalidomide. Eur. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 57, 365–376 (2001)."). Since the report by Blaschke *et al*. used large doses of thalidomide (<200 mg/kg for (*S*)-**1** and <400 mg/kg for (*R*)-**1**)[17](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR17 "Blaschke, G., Kraft, H. P., Fickentscher, K. & Kohler, F. Chromatographic separation of racemic thalidomide and teratogenic activity of its enantiomers. Arzneim.-Forsch. 29, 1640–1642 (1979)."), this phenomenon is even more likely to occur.

Finally, we carried out similar experiments using non-racemic fluoro-thalidomide **2**[30](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR30 "Takeuchi, Y., Shiragami, T., Kimura, K., Suzuki, E. & Shibata, N. (R)- and (S)-3-Fluorothalidomides:  Isosteric Analogues of Thalidomide. Org. Lett. 1, 1571–1573 (1999)."),[37](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR37 "Yamamoto, T., Suzuki, Y., Ito, E., Tokunaga, E. & Shibata, N. Asymmetric Synthesis of Both Mirror Images of 3′-Fluorothalidomide by Enantiodivergent Fluorination Using a Single, Cinchona Alkaloid. Org. Lett. 13, 470–473 (2011)."), which is a non-racemized bioisostere of thalidomide **1**. Compound **2** exhibits potent anti-tumor activity against the human multiple myeloma cell line H929[38](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR38 "Tokunaga, E. et al. Biological evaluation of both enantiomers of fluoro-thalidomide using human myeloma cell line H929 and others. PLoS One 12, e0182152 (2017)."), while it is non-teratogenic[36](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#ref-CR36 "Lee, C. J., Shibata, N., Wiley, M. J. & Wells, P. G. Fluorothalidomide: a characterization of maternal and developmental toxicity in rabbits and mice. Toxicol. Sci. 122, 157–169 (2011)."). We thus briefly optimized the conditions for SDE of non-racemic **2** in water (*cf*. Table [S3](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#MOESM1)) and similar results were observed, i.e., (*S*)-**2** (23% ee) was initially dissolved in DMSO, before water was added. After vigorous stirring for 1 h at room temperature, the supernatant of the suspension contained <76% ee of (*S*)-**2**, while a substantial amount of precipitate was observed (Fig. [7](/articles/s41598-018-35457-6#Fig7)).

![figure 7](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-018-35457-6/MediaObjects/41598_2018_35457_Fig7_HTML.png)

Self-disproportionation of non-racemic **2** in water. (*S*)-**2** (23% ee, 5 mg) was dissolved in DMSO, and the SDE experiment was carried out upon addition of water. The final ee was determined from the supernatant after 1 h of stirring by HPLC using a CHIRALCEL OJ-H column with ethanol as the eluent. A substantial amount of precipitate was also observed.

In summary, we have disclosed the SDE of non-racemic thalidomide (**1**) in water and phosphate buffer solution. (*R*)-**1** (20% ee) undergoes SDE in water and aqueous buffer solution to furnish racemic **1** (as an (*R*/*S*)-heterodimer) in the form of a precipitate and enantiomerically enriched (*R*)-**1** (as the monomer and the (*R*/*R*)-homodimer) in solution (<98% ee). The same SDE was also observed for (*S*)-**1** (20% ee) in the same solvent system.

# Thalidomide

![Image of Thalidomide](/content/dam/acsorg/molecule/archive-new/t/thalidomide-teaser.png)
![3D Image of Thalidomide](/content/dam/acsorg/molecule/archive-new/t/thalidomide-3d.png)

Thalidomide has a tragic history: It was introduced in Germany in 1957 as a sedative and hypnotic and was marketed over the counter largely as a drug for treating morning sickness in pregnant women. In the following few years, about 10,000 infants worldwide were born with phocomelia, or limb malformation. Only half of the infants survived, and some of those who did had other defects in addition to limb deficiencies. The thalidomide disaster caused many countries to tighten drug approval regulations.

Thalidomide exists in two mirror-image forms: it is a racemic mixture of (*R*)- and (*S*)-enantiomers. The (*R*)-enantiomer, shown in the figure, has sedative effects, whereas the (*S*)-isomer is teratogenic. Under biological conditions, the isomers interconvert, so separating the isomers before use is ineffective.

More recently, thalidomide has proven useful for treating cancer and leprosy and is approved for these uses. But although more than 2000 papers have been written about its mechanism of teratogenic action, it was not until the past few years that this mechanism was established. In 2010, H. Handa and colleagues at the Tokyo Institute of Technology showed that its biological target is cereblon, a component of an E3 ubiquitin ligase complex. Earlier this year, N. H. Thomä  and co-workers at the Friedrich Miescher Institute for Biomedical Research (Basel, Switzerland) [determined the crystal structure](http://cen.acs.org/articles/92/i29/Mechanism-Thalidomide-Found.html) of thalidomide bound to cereblon, which allowed them to characterize the mechanism.

*Thalidomide was also the Molecule of the Week for May 17, 2010.*

### MOTW update: March 11, 2024

**Thalidomide**1 is a drug introduced in the 1950s primarily for treating morning sickness in pregnant women. Tragically, it turned out to be a teratogen that caused about 10,000 infants worldwide to be born with phocomelia, or limb malformation. In later years, thalidomide was approved for treating cancer and leprosy.

In a recently published book, [*The Malta Thalidomide Affair*](https://bdlbooks.com/product/the-malta-thalidomide-affair/), author Anatole Baldacchino, a prominent Maltese certified public accountant and member of several government boards of directors, writes about how slowly and poorly the Maltese government responded to the thalidomide crisis in that country. (Baldacchino has a personal stake in this story; he was born with relatively mild phocomelia.) The catastrophe came at a time when Malta was experiencing political, social, and religious upheaval. In January, *The Dispatch*, an American online magazine, published an article with a brief review of the book plus other troubling information about the [thalidomide scandal](https://cde.news/the-thalidomide-scandal-a-legislative-political-and-regulatory-failure/).

1. CAS Reg. No. 50-35-1 (racemic).

--- SOURCE 5: Thalidomide - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thalidomide

SUMMARY:
The birth defects caused by thalidomide led to the development of greater drug regulation and monitoring in many countries. Because of thalidomide's potential for causing birth defects, the drug may be distributed only under tightly controlled conditions. The conditions required under the program include limiting prescription and dispensing rights to authorized prescribers and pharmacies only, keeping a registry of all patients prescribed thalidomide, providing extensive patient education about the risks associated with the drug, and providing periodic pregnancy tests for women who take the drug. After further work was done by Celgene and others, in 2006 the US Food and Drug Administration granted accelerated approval for thalidomide in combination with dexamethasone for the treatment of newly diagnosed multiple myeloma patients.

**Thalidomide**, sold under the brand names **Contergan** and **Thalomid** among others, is an [oral administered](/wiki/Oral_administered "Oral administered") medication used to treat a number of cancers (e.g., [multiple myeloma](/wiki/Multiple_myeloma "Multiple myeloma")), [graft-versus-host disease](/wiki/Graft-versus-host_disease "Graft-versus-host disease"), and many [skin disorders](/wiki/Skin_disorder "Skin disorder") (e.g., complications of [leprosy](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy") such as [skin lesions](/wiki/Skin_lesion "Skin lesion")).[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS-6)[[7]](#cite_note-pubchem-7) Thalidomide has been used to treat conditions associated with [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV"): [aphthous ulcers](/wiki/Aphthous_ulcers "Aphthous ulcers"), HIV-associated [wasting syndrome](/wiki/Wasting_syndrome "Wasting syndrome"), diarrhea, and [Kaposi's sarcoma](/wiki/Kaposi%27s_sarcoma "Kaposi's sarcoma"), but increases in HIV viral load have been reported.[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS-6)

Common side effects include [sleepiness](/wiki/Sleepiness "Sleepiness"), [rash](/wiki/Rash "Rash"), and [dizziness](/wiki/Dizziness "Dizziness").[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS-6) Severe side effects include [tumor lysis syndrome](/wiki/Tumor_lysis_syndrome "Tumor lysis syndrome"), [blood clots](/wiki/Blood_clots "Blood clots"), and [peripheral neuropathy](/wiki/Peripheral_neuropathy "Peripheral neuropathy").[[8]](#cite_note-BNF76-8) Thalidomide is a known human [teratogen](/wiki/Teratogen "Teratogen") and carries an extremely high risk of severe, life-threatening birth defects if administered or taken during pregnancy.[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS-6) It causes skeletal deformities such as [amelia](/wiki/Amelia_(birth_defect) "Amelia (birth defect)") (absence of legs and/or arms), absence of bones, and [phocomelia](/wiki/Phocomelia "Phocomelia") (malformation of the limbs). A single dose of thalidomide, regardless of dosage, is enough to cause [teratogenic](/wiki/Teratogenic "Teratogenic") effects.[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS-6)

Thalidomide was first marketed in 1957 in West Germany, where it was available as an [over-the-counter drug](/wiki/Over-the-counter_drug "Over-the-counter drug").[[9]](#cite_note-OUP2003-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Mill1991-10) When first released, thalidomide was promoted for [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"), [trouble sleeping](/wiki/Insomnia "Insomnia"), "tension", and [morning sickness](/wiki/Morning_sickness "Morning sickness").[[10]](#cite_note-Mill1991-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Lou2004-11) While it was initially thought to be safe in pregnancy, [thalidomide was found to cause birth defects](/wiki/Thalidomide_scandal "Thalidomide scandal"), resulting in its removal from the market in Europe in 1961.[[9]](#cite_note-OUP2003-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Mill1991-10) The total number of infants severely harmed by thalidomide use during pregnancy is estimated at over 10,000, possibly 20,000, of whom about 40% died around the time of birth.[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS-6)[[10]](#cite_note-Mill1991-10) Those who survived had limb, eye, urinary tract, and heart problems.[[9]](#cite_note-OUP2003-9) Its initial entry into the US market was prevented by [Frances Kelsey](/wiki/Frances_Kelsey "Frances Kelsey"), a reviewer at the FDA.[[11]](#cite_note-Lou2004-11) The birth defects caused by thalidomide led to the development of greater [drug regulation](/wiki/Drug_regulation "Drug regulation") and monitoring in many countries.[[9]](#cite_note-OUP2003-9)[[11]](#cite_note-Lou2004-11)

It was approved in the United States in 1998 for use as a treatment for cancer.[[6]](#cite_note-AHFS-6) It is on the [World Health Organization's List of Essential Medicines](/wiki/World_Health_Organization%27s_List_of_Essential_Medicines "World Health Organization's List of Essential Medicines").[[12]](#cite_note-WHO22nd-12) It is available as a [generic medication](/wiki/Generic_medication "Generic medication").[[8]](#cite_note-BNF76-8)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Medical uses

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Pack_of_Thalidomide_tablets.jpg/250px-Pack_of_Thalidomide_tablets.jpg)

Thalidomide is used as a first-line treatment for [multiple myeloma](/wiki/Multiple_myeloma "Multiple myeloma") in combination with [dexamethasone](/wiki/Dexamethasone "Dexamethasone") or with [melphalan](/wiki/Melphalan "Melphalan") and [prednisone](/wiki/Prednisone "Prednisone") to treat acute episodes of [erythema nodosum leprosum](/wiki/Erythema_nodosum_leprosum "Erythema nodosum leprosum"), as well as for maintenance therapy.[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)

The bacterium that causes [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") (TB) is related to [leprosy](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy"). Thalidomide may be helpful in some cases where [standard TB drugs](/wiki/Tuberculosis_management "Tuberculosis management") and [corticosteroids](/wiki/Corticosteroid "Corticosteroid") are not sufficient to resolve severe inflammation in the brain.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

It is used as a second-line treatment to manage [graft-versus-host disease](/wiki/Graft-versus-host_disease "Graft-versus-host disease") and [aphthous stomatitis](/wiki/Aphthous_stomatitis "Aphthous stomatitis") in children and has been prescribed for other conditions in children, including [actinic prurigo](/wiki/Actinic_prurigo "Actinic prurigo") and [epidermolysis bullosa](/wiki/Epidermolysis_bullosa "Epidermolysis bullosa"); the evidence for these uses is weak.[[18]](#cite_note-18) It is recommended only as a third line treatment in graft-versus-host-disease in adults because of lack of efficacy and side effects observed in clinical trials.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Contraindications

Prescriptions of thalidomide are accompanied by strict measures to avoid any possibility of use during pregnancy, and thalidomide should be avoided in women wanting to conceive.[[21]](#cite_note-bermas2019a-21) In the United States, the prescribing doctor is required to ensure that [contraception](/wiki/Contraception "Contraception") is being used and that regular pregnancy tests are taken.[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)

## Adverse effects

Thalidomide [causes birth defects](/wiki/Teratogen "Teratogen").[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)[[22]](#cite_note-toxmech2009-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) The U.S. [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/FDA "FDA") (FDA) and other regulatory agencies have approved marketing of the drug only with an auditable [risk evaluation and mitigation strategy](/wiki/Risk_evaluation_and_mitigation_strategy "Risk evaluation and mitigation strategy") that ensures that people using the drug are aware of the risks and avoid pregnancy; this applies to both men and women, as the drug can be transmitted in [semen](/wiki/Semen "Semen").[[22]](#cite_note-toxmech2009-22)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[21]](#cite_note-bermas2019a-21)

There is a high risk that thalidomide can cause excessive [blood clots](/wiki/Thrombosis "Thrombosis"). There is also a high risk that thalidomide can interfere with the production of several types of new blood cells, creating a risk of infection via [neutropenia](/wiki/Neutropenia "Neutropenia"), [leukopenia](/wiki/Leukopenia "Leukopenia"), and [lymphopenia](/wiki/Lymphopenia "Lymphopenia"), and risks that blood will not clot via [thrombocytopenia](/wiki/Thrombocytopenia "Thrombocytopenia"). There is also a risk of [anemia](/wiki/Anemia "Anemia") via lack of red blood cells. The drug can also damage nerves, causing potentially irreversible [peripheral neuropathy](/wiki/Peripheral_neuropathy "Peripheral neuropathy").[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)

Thalidomide has several adverse cardiovascular effects, including risk of [heart attacks](/wiki/Heart_attack "Heart attack"), [pulmonary hypertension](/wiki/Pulmonary_hypertension "Pulmonary hypertension"), and changes in heart rhythm, such as [syncope](/wiki/Syncope_(medicine) "Syncope (medicine)"), [bradycardia](/wiki/Bradycardia "Bradycardia"), and [atrioventricular block](/wiki/Atrioventricular_block "Atrioventricular block").[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)

Thalidomide can cause [liver damage](/wiki/Liver_damage "Liver damage") and severe skin reactions like [Stevens–Johnson syndrome](/wiki/Stevens%E2%80%93Johnson_syndrome "Stevens–Johnson syndrome"). It tends to make people sleepy, which creates risk when driving and operating other machinery. As it kills cancer cells, it can cause [tumor lysis syndrome](/wiki/Tumor_lysis_syndrome "Tumor lysis syndrome"). Thalidomide can [prevent menstruation](/wiki/Amenorrhea "Amenorrhea").[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)

In addition, very common (reported in more than 10% of people) adverse effects include [tremor](/wiki/Tremor "Tremor"), dizziness, tingling, numbness, constipation, and [peripheral edema](/wiki/Peripheral_edema "Peripheral edema").[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)

Common adverse effects (reported by 1–10% of people) include confusion, depressed mood, reduced coordination, heart failure, difficulty breathing, interstitial lung disease, lung inflammation, vomiting, dry mouth, rashes, dry skin, fever, weakness, and a sense of unwellness.[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)

### Interactions

There are no expected [pharmacokinetic](/wiki/Pharmacokinetics "Pharmacokinetics") interactions between thalidomide and other medicines due to its neutral effects on [P-glycoprotein](/wiki/P-glycoprotein "P-glycoprotein") and the [cytochrome P450](/wiki/Cytochrome_P450 "Cytochrome P450") family. It may interact with sedatives due to its sedative action and bradycardic agents, like beta-blockers, due to its bradycardia-inducing effects. The risk of [peripheral neuropathy](/wiki/Peripheral_neuropathy "Peripheral neuropathy") may be increased by concomitant treatment of thalidomide with other agents known to cause peripheral neuropathy.[[24]](#cite_note-TGA-24) The risk of [venous thromboembolisms](/wiki/Venous_thromboembolism "Venous thromboembolism") with thalidomide seems to be increased when patients are treated with [oral contraceptives](/wiki/Oral_contraceptives "Oral contraceptives") or other cytotoxic agents (including [doxorubicin](/wiki/Doxorubicin "Doxorubicin") and [melphalan](/wiki/Melphalan "Melphalan")) concurrently. Thalidomide may interfere with various contraceptives, and hence it is advised that women of reproductive age use at least two different means of contraception to ensure that no child will be conceived while they are taking thalidomide.[[14]](#cite_note-UKlabel2017-14)[[15]](#cite_note-USlabel2017-15)[[24]](#cite_note-TGA-24)

### Overdose

As of 2013, eighteen cases of overdoses had been reported with doses of up to 14.4 grams, none of them fatal.[[24]](#cite_note-TGA-24) No specific antidote for overdose exists and treatment is purely [supportive](/wiki/Supportive_treatment "Supportive treatment").[[24]](#cite_note-TGA-24)

## Pharmacology

The precise mechanism of action for thalidomide was not known until the twenty-first century,[[25]](#cite_note-Yamamoto-25) although efforts to identify thalidomide's [teratogenic](/wiki/Teratology "Teratology") action generated more than 2,000 research papers and the proposal of 15 or 16 plausible mechanisms by 2000.[[26]](#cite_note-Stephens2000-26) The primary mechanism of action of thalidomide and its analogs in both their anti-cancer and teratogenic effects is now known to be as [cereblon](/wiki/Cereblon "Cereblon") [E3 ligase](/wiki/E3_ligase "E3 ligase") modulators.[[25]](#cite_note-Yamamoto-25)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Thalidomide also binds to and acts as an [antagonist](/wiki/Receptor_antagonist "Receptor antagonist") of the [androgen receptor](/wiki/Androgen_receptor "Androgen receptor") and hence is a [nonsteroidal antiandrogen](/wiki/Nonsteroidal_antiandrogen "Nonsteroidal antiandrogen") of some capacity.[[30]](#cite_note-LiuSu2010-30) In accordance, it can produce [gynecomastia](/wiki/Gynecomastia "Gynecomastia") and [sexual dysfunction](/wiki/Sexual_dysfunction "Sexual dysfunction") as side effects in men.[[31]](#cite_note-NuttallWarrier2015-31)

### Chirality and biological activity

Thalidomide is provided as a [racemic mixture](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture") of two [enantiomers](/wiki/Enantiomer "Enantiomer"); while there are reports that only one of the enantiomers may cause birth defects, the body converts each enantiomer into the other through mechanisms that are not well understood.[[22]](#cite_note-toxmech2009-22) The (R)-enantiomer has the desired sedative effect while the (S)-enantiomer harbors embryo-toxic and teratogenic effects. Attempting to extract solely *R*-thalidomide does not remove the risk of birth defects, as it was demonstrated that the "safe" *R*-thalidomide undergoes an *in vivo* [chiral inversion](/wiki/Chiral_inversion "Chiral inversion") to the "teratogenic" *S*-thalidomide. Under biological conditions, the enantiomers interconvert (*bidirectional chiral inversion* – (R)- to (S)- and vice versa).[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Chemistry

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Thalidomide-structures.png/330px-Thalidomide-structures.png)

Thalidomide is [racemic](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture"); while *S*-thalidomide is the bioactive form of the molecule, the individual enantiomers can [racemize](/wiki/Racemization "Racemization") to each other due to the acidic hydrogen at the [chiral centre](/wiki/Chiral_centre "Chiral centre"), which is the carbon of the [glutarimide](/wiki/Glutarimide "Glutarimide") ring bonded to the [phthalimide](/wiki/Phthalimide "Phthalimide") [substituent](/wiki/Substituent "Substituent"). The racemization process can occur *[in vivo](/wiki/In_vivo "In vivo")*.[[5]](#cite_note-clinp-5)[[34]](#cite_note-stereospecific44-34)[[35]](#cite_note-pmid14505639-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Bartlett2004-36) The process of conversion of one enantiomer to its mirror-image version with no other change in the molecule is called chiral inversion.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Celgene Corporation](/wiki/Celgene_Corporation "Celgene Corporation") originally synthesized thalidomide using a three-step sequence starting with [L-glutamic acid](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate") treatment, but this has since been reformed by the use of [L-glutamine](/wiki/Glutamine "Glutamine").[[38]](#cite_note-synth-38) As shown in the image below, *N*-carbethoxyphthalimide (1) can react with L-glutamine to yield *N*-phthaloyl-L-glutamine (2). Cyclization of *N*-phthaloyl-L-glutamine occurs using [carbonyldiimidazole](/wiki/Carbonyldiimidazole "Carbonyldiimidazole"), which then yields thalidomide (3).[[38]](#cite_note-synth-38) Celgene Corporation's original method resulted in a 31% yield of *S*-thalidomide, whereas the two-step synthesis yields 85–93% product that is 99% pure.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2023, it is reported that [phthalic anhydride](/wiki/Phthalic_anhydride "Phthalic anhydride") and [L-glutamine](/wiki/Glutamine "Glutamine") under suitable conditions can react directly to form thalidomide. In the procedure, phthalic anhydride and L-glutamine are grounded and added into [toluene](/wiki/Toluene "Toluene") solvent. The solution, along with [triethylamine](/wiki/Triethylamine "Triethylamine") and [acetic anhydride](/wiki/Acetic_anhydride "Acetic anhydride"), is refluxed at ~110°C for 9 hours; after that the solution goes through a simple [vacuum filtration](/wiki/Vacuum_filtration "Vacuum filtration") procedure to obtain the product.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Thalidomide_syntehsis.png/960px-Thalidomide_syntehsis.png)

## History

In 1952, thalidomide was synthesised by [Chemical Industry Basel](/wiki/Novartis#Ciba-Geigy "Novartis"), but was found "to have no effect on animals" and was discarded on that basis.[[40]](#cite_note-themee-40) In 1957, it was acquired by [Chemie Grünenthal](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCnenthal "Grünenthal") in Germany.[[40]](#cite_note-themee-40) The German company had been established as a soap maker after World War II ended, to address the urgent market need for antibiotics.[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Heinrich Mückter](/wiki/Heinrich_M%C3%BCckter "Heinrich Mückter")[[42]](#cite_note-ktnyt-42) was appointed to head the discovery program based on his experience working with the German army's antiviral research. While preparing [reagents](/wiki/Reagents "Reagents") for the work, Mueckter's assistant Wilhelm Kunz isolated a by-product that was recognized by pharmacologist Herbert Keller as an analog of [glutethimide](/wiki/Glutethimide "Glutethimide"), a [sedative](/wiki/Sedative "Sedative"). The medicinal chemistry work turned to improving the lead compound into a suitable drug: the result was thalidomide. The toxicity was examined in several animals, and the drug was introduced in 1956 as a sedative, but it was never tested on pregnant women.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Researchers at Chemie Grünenthal found that thalidomide was a particularly effective [antiemetic](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic") that had an inhibitory effect on [morning sickness](/wiki/Morning_sickness "Morning sickness").[[44]](#cite_note-pmid15172781-44) On 1 October 1957, the company launched thalidomide and began marketing it under the trade name Contergan.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-Bombay-46) It was proclaimed a "wonder drug" for [insomnia](/wiki/Insomnia "Insomnia"), coughs, colds and headaches.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

During that period, the use of medications during pregnancy was not strictly controlled, and drugs were not thoroughly tested for potential harm to the [fetus](/wiki/Fetus "Fetus").[[44]](#cite_note-pmid15172781-44) Thousands of pregnant women took the drug to relieve their symptoms. At the time of the drug's development, scientists did not believe any drug taken by a pregnant woman could pass across the [placental barrier](/wiki/Placental_barrier "Placental barrier") and harm the developing fetus.[[48]](#cite_note-Chem1994-48) There soon appeared reports of abnormalities in children being born to mothers using thalidomide. In late 1959, it was noticed that [peripheral neuritis](/wiki/Peripheral_neuritis "Peripheral neuritis") developed in patients who took the drug over a period of time, and it was only after this point that thalidomide ceased to be provided over the counter.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

While initially considered safe, the drug was responsible for [teratogenic](/wiki/Teratogenic "Teratogenic") deformities in children born after their mothers used it during pregnancies, prior to the third trimester. In November 1961, thalidomide was taken off the market due to massive pressure from the press and public.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Experts estimate that thalidomide led to the death of approximately 2,000 children and serious birth defects in more than 10,000 children, with over half of them in West Germany.[[51]](#cite_note-Thalidomide-induced_teratogenesis-51) The regulatory authorities in [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") never approved thalidomide.[[52]](#cite_note-oncozine.com-52) One reason for the initially unobserved side effects of the drug and the subsequent approval in West Germany was that at that time drugs did not have to be tested for teratogenic effects. They were tested for toxicity on rodents only, as was usual at the time.[[53]](#cite_note-VFA-53)

In the UK, the British pharmaceutical company [The Distillers Company](/wiki/The_Distillers_Company "The Distillers Company") (Biochemicals) Ltd, a subsidiary of Distillers Co. Ltd (now part of [Diageo plc](/wiki/Diageo_plc "Diageo plc")), marketed thalidomide throughout the UK, Australia, and New Zealand, under the brand name Distaval, as a remedy for [morning sickness](/wiki/Morning_sickness "Morning sickness"). Their advertisement claimed that "Distaval can be given with complete safety to pregnant women and nursing mothers without adverse effect on mother or child ... Outstandingly safe Distaval has been prescribed for nearly three years in this country."[[52]](#cite_note-oncozine.com-52) Globally, more pharmaceutical companies started to produce and market the drug under license from Chemie Grünenthal. Thalidomide was available in 46 countries under many different brand names.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In the US, representatives from Chemie Grünenthal approached [Smith, Kline & French](/wiki/Smith,_Kline_%26_French "Smith, Kline & French") (SKF), now [GlaxoSmithKline](/wiki/GlaxoSmithKline "GlaxoSmithKline"), with a request to market and distribute the drug in North America. A memorandum, rediscovered in 2010 in the archives of the FDA, shows that in 1956–57, as part of its in-licensing approach, Smith, Kline and French conducted animal tests and ran a clinical trial of the drug in the US involving 875 people, including pregnant women.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In 1956, researchers involved in clinical trials at SKF noted that, even when used in very high doses, thalidomide could not induce sleep in mice.[[56]](#cite_note-56) When administered at doses 50 to 650 times larger than that claimed by Chemie Grünenthal to be "sleep-inducing", the researchers could still not achieve the hypnotic effect in animals that it had on humans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After completion of the trial, and based on reasons kept hidden for decades, SKF declined to commercialize the drug. In 1958, Chemie Grünenthal reached an agreement with the William S. Merrell Company in Cincinnati, Ohio ([later Richardson-Merrell](/wiki/Marion_Merrell_Dow#Richardson-Merrell "Marion Merrell Dow"), now part of [Sanofi](/wiki/Sanofi "Sanofi")), to market and distribute thalidomide throughout the US.[[52]](#cite_note-oncozine.com-52)

The US FDA refused to approve thalidomide for marketing and distribution. However, the drug was distributed in large quantities for testing purposes, after the American distributor and manufacturer [Richardson-Merrell](/wi

enriched_points.expanded_content: Thalidomide's racemic mixture contains both enantiomers, with only the S-enantiomer causing teratogenic effects. This led to severe birth defects, highlighting the dangers of using racemic drugs.

Ibuprofen exemplifies the significance of chiral synthesis as its S-enantiomer provides anti-inflammatory effects, while the R-enantiomer is less effective, emphasizing enantiomer-specific drug efficacy.

S(-)-Propranolol is the active enantiomer for treating hypertension and anxiety, while R(+)-Propranolol lacks significant therapeutic effects, showcasing the critical role of chirality in pharmacology.
